one_line_takeaway: HAMSA automates stealthy, RAG-augmented evolutionary jailbreak generation with a policy-file disguise template, improving success rates and reducing attempts over an AutoDAN baseline on 10/13 topics across three 7B models, and shows Arabic (Darija) jailbreaks are markedly more potent (mean violation score 2.24) than English ones (1.28) against a Gigachat-family target.
key_finding: AutoDAN-Boost (the paper's method) outperformed the AutoDAN baseline on all three metrics (Absolute success rate, Mean quality, and Num=average attempts needed) for 10 of 13 topics across all three attacker models (Mistral, Qwen, Vicuna), per Table 2. Cited examples from the text: Financial Advice with Mistral improved from an Absolute rate of 0.23 to 0.37 (about 60% relative improvement) while attempts dropped from 4.53 to 4.47; Hate Speech with Qwen improved roughly 80% (Absolute 0.50 to 0.90) while attempts dropped from 3.83 to 2.80. Vicuna's already-strong baseline on Fraud (0.90) and Illegal Activity (0.87) was pushed to perfect 1.00 success by the boosted variant while keeping Mean quality above 9.40 and reducing attempt counts. Cross-lingual finding: mean safety-violation score of harmful outputs was 1.28 for English versus 2.24 for Arabic (Darija), indicating higher adversarial potency / weaker alignment robustness for the low-resource Darija dialect; the authors attribute this to reduced alignment-training coverage for the dialect and to Arabic morphological/idiomatic structure enabling easier disguise of adversarial instructions.
core_contribution: The paper introduces HAMSA, an automated red-teaming framework that evolves semantically coherent, stealthy jailbreak prompts against safety-aligned compact LLMs. HAMSA combines three original elements: (1) a two-phase 'WarmUp' then 'LifeLong' evolutionary search where a small attacking LLM iteratively refines prompts, scored by an evaluator LLM and, on success, stored in a growing RAG strategy library; (2) a 'Policy Puppetry Template' that disguises the malicious instruction inside a benign-looking structured config file (XML, INI, or JSON) to defeat token-level/perplexity-based filters while preserving fluency; and (3) a RAG-based lifelong-adaptation mechanism (Algorithm 1) that retrieves the top-K most similar past successful strategies via cosine similarity over response embeddings and adaptively decides whether to reuse, combine, or explore new strategies based on score-differential thresholds (>5, 2-5, <2). The authors also release a new Arabic (Moroccan Darija) jailbreak benchmark of 100 prompts, described as the first such benchmark for that dialect, derived from the English 'In-The-Wild Jailbreak Prompts' dataset.
threat_model: An adversary augments a set of malicious questions Q with jailbreak prompts J to form combined queries T=<J,Q> submitted to a target LLM M, aiming to maximize the probability that the model's response R contains harmful content aligned with the malicious intent rather than a refusal. Success is defined operationally: a response is a 'successful attack' if it does not contain refusal markers from a predefined set and is validated by an evaluator LLM (Gigachat-MAX) using a 10-point safety-violation scale, with score > 8.5 counted as a successful jailbreak. The paper formalizes red-teaming as a discrete optimization problem (Eq. 3) and the iterative attack process as an evolutionary refinement Jt+1 = sigma(mu(Jt), S) (Eq. 4), with the Puppetry Attack instantiated as Ti = <Pi(Ji), Qi> where Pi(.) is a structural (XML/INI/JSON) disguise transform. This is a white/gray-box red-teaming threat model targeting compact (7B-scale) aligned models, run by the paper's own authors as offensive researchers rather than a described real-world attacker.
limitations: The paper explicitly states test-time strategy library is frozen and the summarizer LLM disabled during evaluation, which is a methodological control but also means reported gains reflect a library built from the same distribution the evaluation draws from (WarmUp+LifeLong prompts are all sourced from the same 390-prompt In-The-Wild dataset), raising a generalization/transfer question not addressed for out-of-distribution harmful queries. Success/scoring relies entirely on an LLM judge (Gigachat-MAX) using refusal-marker heuristics and a 10-point scale with an arbitrary 8.5 threshold; no human validation of judge accuracy is reported in the paper. The comparison baseline and the method itself are confusingly both referred to via the label 'AutoDAN'/'AutoDAN-Boost' in Table 2 despite the paper's own name being HAMSA elsewhere in the text -- this internal naming inconsistency was observed directly in the PDF and was not resolved by further reading of the results/discussion sections. Defensive target model is limited to one family (GigachatLite/Gigachat-MAX), so cross-model generalization of the attack beyond the tested triad is not evaluated. The Darija dataset (100 prompts) is comparatively small and drawn from translations of the same source dataset rather than independently elicited harmful queries.

datasets_used: In-The-Wild Jailbreak Prompts on LLMs (390 prompts, 13 safety-critical topics)
Newly curated Moroccan Darija Arabic jailbreak dataset (100 prompts, derived/translated from In-The-Wild Jailbreak Prompts)
quantitative_results: AutoDAN-Boost beat AutoDAN baseline on Absolute success rate, Mean quality, and Num attempts in 10 of 13 topics across all three attacker models
Financial Advice (Mistral): Absolute success rate 0.23 -> 0.37 (~60% relative improvement); attempts 4.53 -> 4.47
Hate Speech (Qwen): Absolute success rate 0.50 -> 0.90 (~80% relative improvement); attempts 3.83 -> 2.80
Vicuna Fraud: Absolute 0.90 -> 1.00; Vicuna Illegal Activity: Absolute 0.87 -> 1.00; Privacy Violence: Absolute 0.77 -> 1.00, all with Mean quality kept above 9.40
Mean safety-violation score: English 1.28 vs Arabic (Darija) 2.24

WarmUp phase: 20 seed prompts, single-pass; LifeLong phase: 170 prompts (43.6% of 390) stratified sample, 3 cycles, 5 iterative refinements each; final evaluation: all 390 prompts, 5 independent attack iterations each, success threshold score > 8.5 on a 10-point scale
baselines_compared: AutoDAN (Liu et al., cited as [35]) -- baseline jailbreak generation method compared against the paper's boosted variant in Table 2
